([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Viejo Sendero Español*) is a historic [trade route](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") that connected the [Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") settlements of (or near) [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") with those of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and the rest of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"). Approximately 700 mi (1,100 km) long, the trail ran through areas of high mountains, arid deserts, and deep canyons. It is considered one of the most arduous of all trade routes ever established in the United States. Explored, in part, by Spanish explorers as early as the late 16th century, the trail was extensively used by traders with [pack trains](/wiki/Pack_train "Pack train") from about 1830 until the mid-1850s. The area was part of Mexico from [Mexican independence](/wiki/Mexican_independence "Mexican independence") in 1821 to the [Mexican Cession](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession") to the United States in 1848.

The **Old Spanish Trail** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Viejo Sendero Español*) is a historic [trade route](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") that connected the [Nuevo México](/wiki/Santa_Fe_de_Nuevo_M%C3%A9xico "Santa Fe de Nuevo México") settlements of (or near) [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") with those of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") and the rest of [Alta California](/wiki/Alta_California "Alta California"). Approximately 700 mi (1,100 km) long, the trail ran through areas of high mountains, arid deserts, and deep canyons. It is considered one of the most arduous of all trade routes ever established in the United States. Explored, in part, by Spanish explorers as early as the late 16th century, the trail was extensively used by traders with [pack trains](/wiki/Pack_train "Pack train") from about 1830 until the mid-1850s. The area was part of Mexico from [Mexican independence](/wiki/Mexican_independence "Mexican independence") in 1821 to the [Mexican Cession](/wiki/Mexican_Cession "Mexican Cession") to the United States in 1848.

The name of the trail comes from the publication of [John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont")'s Report of his 1844 journey (which crossed into Mexico) for the [U.S. Topographical Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Topographical_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Topographical Engineers"), guided by [Kit Carson](/wiki/Kit_Carson "Kit Carson"), from California to New Mexico. The name acknowledges that parts of the trail had been known and used by the [Spanish](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain "Viceroyalty of New Spain") since the 16th century.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) Frémont's report identified a trail that had already been used for about 15 years. The trail is important to New Mexico history because it established an arduous but usable trade route with California.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 2002 this trail was designated by Congress as part of the [National Trails System](/wiki/National_Trails_System "National Trails System") as **Old Spanish National Historic Trail**.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Old_Spanish_Trail_-_Early_Exploration.png/960px-Old_Spanish_Trail_-_Early_Exploration.png)](/wiki/File:Old_Spanish_Trail_-_Early_Exploration.png)

Timeline: Early exploration of the Old Spanish Trail (National Park Service)

The trail is a combination of a network of trails first established by indigenous people and later used by Spanish explorers, trappers, and traders with the [Ute](/wiki/Ute_tribe "Ute tribe") and other indigenous tribes. The eastern parts of what became called the Old Spanish Trail, including southwest [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") and southeast [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), were explored by [Juan Maria de Rivera](/wiki/Juan_Rivera_(explorer) "Juan Rivera (explorer)") in 1765. [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") missionaries [Francisco Atanasio Domínguez](/wiki/Francisco_Atanasio_Dom%C3%ADnguez "Francisco Atanasio Domínguez") and [Silvestre Vélez de Escalante](/wiki/Silvestre_V%C3%A9lez_de_Escalante "Silvestre Vélez de Escalante") unsuccessfully attempted the trip to [California](/wiki/Las_Californias "Las Californias"), which was just being settled, leaving Santa Fe in 1776 and making it to the [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin") near [Utah Lake](/wiki/Utah_Lake "Utah Lake") before returning via the [Arizona Strip](/wiki/Arizona_Strip "Arizona Strip"). Other expeditions, under another Franciscan missionary, [Francisco Garcés](/wiki/Francisco_Garc%C3%A9s "Francisco Garcés"), and Captain [Juan Bautista de Anza](/wiki/Juan_Bautista_de_Anza "Juan Bautista de Anza"), explored and traded in the southern part of the region. They found shorter and less arduous routes through the mountains and deserts that connected Sonora to New Mexico and California, but these did not become part of the Old Spanish Trail, with the exception of some of the paths through the [Mojave Desert](/wiki/Mojave_Desert "Mojave Desert").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The [Mohave Trail](/wiki/Mohave_Trail "Mohave Trail") was first traveled by Garcés from the [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") villages on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") westward across the Mojave Desert, between desert springs, until he turned northwestward to the [Old Tejon Pass](/wiki/Old_Tejon_Pass "Old Tejon Pass") into the [San Joaquin Valley](/wiki/San_Joaquin_Valley "San Joaquin Valley"), looking for a route to Monterey. Garcés returned to the Colorado River by following the whole length of the Mohave Trail from the [San Bernardino Valley](/wiki/San_Bernardino_Valley "San Bernardino Valley"), over the [San Bernardino Mountains](/wiki/San_Bernardino_Mountains "San Bernardino Mountains") at [Monument Peak](/wiki/Monument_Peak_(San_Bernardino_County) "Monument Peak (San Bernardino County)"), down the [Mojave River](/wiki/Mojave_River "Mojave River") and eastward to the Colorado River. This same trail was used by the first Americans to reach California by land, via the expedition led by [Jedediah Smith](/wiki/Jedediah_Smith "Jedediah Smith") in November 1826.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The Mojave desert section of the Mohave Trail is now a 4WD trail called the [Mojave Road](/wiki/Mojave_Road "Mojave Road").

A route linking [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") to [California](/wiki/California "California"), combining information from many explorers, was opened in 1829-30 when [Santa Fe](/wiki/Santa_Fe,_New_Mexico "Santa Fe, New Mexico") merchant [Antonio Armijo](/wiki/Antonio_Armijo "Antonio Armijo") led a trade party of 60 men and a caravan of mules to Alta California. Armijo's group blazed a trade route using a network of indigenous routes, incorporating parts of Jedediah Smith's routes of 1826 and 1827, and Rafael Rivera's route of 1828 to the [San Gabriel Mission](/wiki/Mission_San_Gabriel_Arc%C3%A1ngel "Mission San Gabriel Arcángel") through the Mojave along the [Mojave River](/wiki/Mojave_River "Mojave River"). Armijo documented his route in a report to the governor, and this was published by the Mexican government in June 1830.[[6]](#cite_note-Armijo-6)

After this date, traders generally used the trail for a single, annual round trip.[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7) Word spread about Armijo's successful trade expedition, and some commerce began between Santa Fe and Los Angeles. However, in 1830, due to resumed hostilities with the [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo"), the Armijo route west to the Colorado River [Crossing of the Fathers](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Fathers "Crossing of the Fathers") was not practical. A new route north of the river had to be found, which used the trails of the fur traders and trappers of New Mexico through the lands of the Ute. This route ran northwest to the Colorado and [Green](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)") rivers, then crossed over to the [Sevier River](/wiki/Sevier_River "Sevier River"), which it followed until crossing westward over mountains to the vicinity of [Parowan, Utah](/wiki/Parowan,_Utah "Parowan, Utah"). It passed southward to the Santa Clara River, linking up with Armijo's route to California.

This commerce usually consisted of one mule pack train from Santa Fe with 20 to 200 members, with roughly twice as many mules, bringing New Mexican goods hand-woven by Indians, such as serapes and blankets, to California. California had many horses and mules, many growing wild, with no local market, which were readily traded for hand-woven Indian products. Usually two blankets were traded for one horse; more blankets were usually required for a mule, which were considered hardier. California had almost no wool-processing industry and few weavers, so woven products were a welcome commodity. The trading party usually left New Mexico in early November to take advantage of winter rains to cross the deserts on the trail and would arrive in California in early February. The return party would usually leave California for New Mexico in early April to get over the trail before the water holes dried up and the melting snow raised the rivers too high. The return party often drove several hundred to a few thousand horses and mules.

Low-scale emigration from New Mexico to California used parts of the trail in the late 1830s when the trapping trade began to die. New Mexicans migrated to settle in Alta California by this route: some first settled in [Politana](/wiki/Politana,_California "Politana, California") then established the twin settlements of [Agua Mansa](/wiki/Agua_Mansa,_California "Agua Mansa, California") and [La Placita](/wiki/La_Placita,_California "La Placita, California") on the [Santa Ana River](/wiki/Santa_Ana_River "Santa Ana River") the first towns in what became [San Bernardino](/wiki/San_Bernardino_County,_California "San Bernardino County, California") and [Riverside](/wiki/Riverside_County,_California "Riverside County, California") counties. The family of Antonio Armijo moved to Alta California, where his father acquired the [Rancho Tolenas](/wiki/Rancho_Tolenas "Rancho Tolenas"). A number of Americans, most naturalized Mexican citizens in New Mexico, and formerly in the California trade over the Old Spanish Trail or in the fur trade, settled in Alta California. Several became influential residents in later years, such as [Louis Rubidoux](/wiki/Louis_Rubidoux "Louis Rubidoux"), [John A. Rowland](/wiki/John_A._Rowland "John A. Rowland"), [William Workman](/wiki/Workman-Temple_family#William_Workman "Workman-Temple family"), [Benjamin Davis Wilson](/wiki/Benjamin_Davis_Wilson "Benjamin Davis Wilson"), and [William Wolfskill](/wiki/William_Wolfskill "William Wolfskill").

The trail was also used for illicit purposes. Some raiders attacked the California ranchos for horses and captives to sell in the extensive Indian slave trade. Mexicans, ex-trappers and Indian tribes, primarily the [Utes](/wiki/Ute_people "Ute people"), all participated in the horse raiding. With allies, [Walkara](/wiki/Walkara "Walkara") was known to steal hundreds to thousands of horses in a single raid. Native Americans along the route were at risk of being taken captive, especially the women and children of the [Paiute](/wiki/Southern_Paiute "Southern Paiute"), who were sold as domestic servants to Mexican ranchers and other settlers in both California and New Mexico. Mexican traders and Indian raiding parties both participated in this slave trade. The consequences of this human trafficking had a long-standing effect for those who lived along the trail, even after the trail was no longer in use. Intermittent Indian warfare along the trail often resulted from such slave raids by unscrupulous traders and raiding Indians.

[John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont"), "The Great Pathfinder", took the route, guided by [Kit Carson](/wiki/Kit_Carson "Kit Carson"), in 1844 and named it in his report published in 1845. The New Mexico-California trade continued until the mid-1850s, when a shift to the use of freight wagons and the development of wagon trails made the old pack trail route obsolete. By 1846 both New Mexico and California had been annexed as U.S. territories following its victory in the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") of 1846–1848. After 1848 numerous [Mormon](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon") immigrants began settling in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), and [California](/wiki/California "California") all along the trail, affecting both trade interests and tolerance for the slavery of American Natives.[[8]](#cite_note-npsstudy-8)

*Place names used in this article refer to present-day states and communities. Few (if any) settlements existed along the trail, except in the coastal plains of Alta California, before 1850, although many of the geologic features along the Trail retain their Spanish designations.*

## Description of the trail route

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description of the trail route")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Old_Spanish_National_Historic_Trail_%28National_Park_Service%29.png/960px-Old_Spanish_National_Historic_Trail_%28National_Park_Service%29.png)](/wiki/File:Old_Spanish_National_Historic_Trail_(National_Park_Service).png)

Old Spanish Trail Historic Routes (National Park Service)

### Armijo Route

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Armijo Route")]

The Armijo Route of the Old Spanish Trail was established by an expedition led by Antonio Armijo in 1829–1830. Leaving [Abiquiu](/wiki/Abiqui%C3%BA,_New_Mexico "Abiquiú, New Mexico") on November 7, 1829, Armijo's expedition traveled a route northwest and west of Santa Fe, following the [Chama River](/wiki/Chama_River_(Rio_Grande) "Chama River (Rio Grande)") and the [Puerco River](/wiki/Rio_Puerco_(Rio_Chama) "Rio Puerco (Rio Chama)"). He crossed to the [San Juan River](/wiki/San_Juan_River_(Colorado_River) "San Juan River (Colorado River)") basin. From the San Juan, they entered the [Four Corners](/wiki/Four_Corners "Four Corners") area, and passed north of the [Carrizo Mountains](/wiki/Carrizo_Mountains "Carrizo Mountains") to [Church Rock](/wiki/Church_Rock_(Arizona) "Church Rock (Arizona)"), east of present-day [Kayenta](/wiki/Kayenta,_Arizona "Kayenta, Arizona"). The trail ran to [Marsh Pass](/wiki/Marsh_Pass "Marsh Pass") and north through [Tsegi Canyon](/wiki/Tsegi_Canyon "Tsegi Canyon") into canyon country. At the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") (then called the Rio Grande), the travelers forded at the [Crossing of the Fathers](/wiki/Crossing_of_the_Fathers "Crossing of the Fathers") above present-day [Glen Canyon Dam](/wiki/Glen_Canyon_Dam "Glen Canyon Dam").

Continuing west to [Pipe Spring](/wiki/Pipe_Spring_National_Monument "Pipe Spring National Monument") and on to [Virgin River](/wiki/Virgin_River "Virgin River") above present-day [St. George, Utah](/wiki/St._George,_Utah "St. George, Utah"), the expedition followed the Virgin to the mouth of the [Santa Clara River](/wiki/Santa_Clara_River_(Utah) "Santa Clara River (Utah)"), which they followed up to the vicinity of the [Shivwits Reservation](/wiki/Shivwits_Band_of_Paiutes "Shivwits Band of Paiutes"). They crossed southward over the [Beaver Dam Mountains](/wiki/Beaver_Dam_Mountains "Beaver Dam Mountains"), at [Utah Hill Summit](/wiki/Utah_Hill_Summit "Utah Hill Summit") to the Virgin River again, which they followed for three days down to the Colorado River. They traveled west parallel to the river, over difficult terrain in the [Black Mountains](/wiki/Black_Mountains_(Nevada) "Black Mountains (Nevada)"), to avoid the deep narrow gorge of [Boulder Canyon](/wiki/Boulder_Canyon_(Colorado_River) "Boulder Canyon (Colorado River)"), to the riverside [oases](/wiki/Oasis "Oasis") of [Callville Wash](/wiki/Callville_Wash "Callville Wash") and [Las Vegas Wash](/wiki/Las_Vegas_Wash "Las Vegas Wash"). Armijo waited there for his scouts to return, especially Rivera who had visited the Mohave villages downriver before. Rivera returned, having recognized the Mohave Trail that led westward to Southern California. Perhaps because the Mohave had been antagonistic to parties of mountain men in recent years, or to save time, Armijo attempted a short cut route southwest to the mouth of the Mojave River.

From Las Vegas Wash on the Colorado River, Armijo's expedition passed southwestward to [Eldorado Dry Lake](/wiki/Eldorado_Dry_Lake "Eldorado Dry Lake") in [Eldorado Valley](/wiki/Eldorado_Valley "Eldorado Valley") and the spring at [Goodsprings Valley](/wiki/Goodsprings_Valley "Goodsprings Valley"), then through [Wilson Pass](/wiki/Wilson_Pass_(Clark_County,_Nevada) "Wilson Pass (Clark County, Nevada)"), across [Mesquite Valley](/wiki/Mesquite_Valley "Mesquite Valley") and [California Valley](/wiki/California_Valley_(Inyo_County) "California Valley (Inyo County)"), through what became known as [Emigrant Pass](/wiki/Emigrant_Pass_(Nopah_Range) "Emigrant Pass (Nopah Range)") to [Resting Springs](/wiki/Resting_Springs "Resting Springs"), then along the [Amargosa River](/wiki/Amargosa_River "Amargosa River") from near [Tecopa](/wiki/Tecopa,_California "Tecopa, California") to [Salt Spring](/wiki/Salt_Spring_Hills "Salt Spring Hills"). From Salt Spring they crossed a two-day-long waterless stretch up [Salt Creek](/wiki/Salt_Creek_(Amargosa_River) "Salt Creek (Amargosa River)") to *Laguna del Milagro* ("Lake of the Miracle") (probably [Silver Lake](/wiki/Silver_Lake_(Mojave) "Silver Lake (Mojave)")), then to [Ojito del Malpais](/wiki/Zzyzx,_California "Zzyzx, California") ("little spring of the badlands") on [Soda Lake](/wiki/Soda_Lake_(San_Bernardino_County) "Soda Lake (San Bernardino County)"). They had another waterless day beyond Soda Lake, where they reached the [Mojave River](/wiki/Mojave_River "Mojave River"), only intermittently dependable for potable water, and the [Mohave Trail](/wiki/Mohave_Trail "Mohave Trail") leading up river.[[9]](#cite_note-ArmijoRevisited-9): 32–33, 72–74

By then short of food, Armijo sent some of his scouts ahead to get more food in the settlement at [San Bernardino de Sena Estancia](/wiki/San_Bernardino_de_Sena_Estancia "San Bernardino de Sena Estancia"). They followed the river for six days (110 miles to its head from the mouth), having to kill a mule or horse each day to eat. Probably at [Summit Valley](/wiki/Summit_Valley_(San_Bernardino_County,_California) "Summit Valley (San Bernardino County, California)") at the top of the river east of [Cajon Pass](/wiki/Cajon_Pass "Cajon Pass"), they met [vaqueros](/wiki/Vaquero "Vaquero") of the San Bernardino de Sena Estancia who had extra food. Armijo did not cross over the mountains by the Mohave Trail route over [Monument Peak](/wiki/Monument_Peak_(San_Bernardino_County) "Monument Peak (San Bernardino County)"), but followed a route he called "Cañon de San Bernardino" from the upper Mojave River west through [Cajon Pass](/wiki/Cajon_Pass "Cajon Pass") and down [Crowder](/wiki/Crowder_Canyon "Crowder Canyon") and [Cajon](/wiki/Cajon_Canyon "Cajon Canyon") canyons to the mouth of Cajon Pass, where the trail reached the coastal plain of [San Bernardino Valley](/wiki/San_Bernardino_Valley "San Bernardino Valley"). This route was undoubtedly known to the vaqueros of San Bernardino Estancia.[[10]](#cite_note-Hafen-10)[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7)

Once through the pass, they turned west along the foot of the [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains "San Gabriel Mountains") for two days to [San Jose Creek](/wiki/San_Jose_Creek_(San_Gabriel_River) "San Jose Creek (San Gabriel River)"); they followed it, crossing the [San Gabriel River](/wiki/San_Gabriel_River_(California) "San Gabriel River (California)") at the [Rancho La Puente](/wiki/Rancho_La_Puente "Rancho La Puente"), and reaching [Mission San Gabriel Arcángel](/wiki/Mission_San_Gabriel_Arc%C3%A1ngel "Mission San Gabriel Arcángel") on January 30, 1830. Armijo used the same route to return to his original town, traveling from March 1 to April 25, 1830.

He submitted a brief journal of his journey (itemizing the days with names of places where camps were made but not quantifying distances) to the government of New Mexico, and it was published by the Mexican government in June 1830.[[6]](#cite_note-Armijo-6)[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7)[[8]](#cite_note-npsstudy-8)[[10]](#cite_note-Hafen-10)

### Main Northern Route

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Main Northern Route")]

The Main Route (also referred to as the Central Route or the Northern Route) of the Old Spanish Trail avoided territory of the [Navajo](/wiki/Navajo "Navajo"), (who had returned to a state of hostilities after Armijo's trip), and the more difficult canyon country traversed by the Armijo Route around the Colorado River. First traveled in 1830 by a party led by [William Wolfskill](/wiki/William_Wolfskill "William Wolfskill") and [George Yount](/wiki/George_C._Yount "George C. Yount"), this route ran northwest from Santa Fe through southwestern [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), past the [San Juan Mountains](/wiki/San_Juan_Mountains "San Juan Mountains"), [Mancos](/wiki/Mancos,_Colorado "Mancos, Colorado"), and [Dove Creek](/wiki/Dove_Creek,_Colorado "Dove Creek, Colorado"), entering Utah near present-day [Monticello](/wiki/Monticello,_Utah "Monticello, Utah"). The trail proceeded north through difficult terrain to [Spanish Valley](/wiki/Spanish_Valley "Spanish Valley") near today's [Moab, Utah](/wiki/Moab,_Utah "Moab, Utah"), where a ferry crossed the deep and wide Colorado River and then turned northwest to a ferry crossing on the similarly sized and dangerous [Green River](/wiki/Green_River_(Colorado_River) "Green River (Colorado River)") near present-day [Green River, Utah](/wiki/Green_River,_Utah "Green River, Utah"). The route then passed through (or around) the [San Rafael Swell](/wiki/San_Rafael_Swell "San Rafael Swell"), the northernmost reach of the Trail. Entering the [Great Basin](/wiki/Great_Basin "Great Basin") in Utah via [Salina Creek Canyon](/wiki/Salina_Creek_(Sevier_River) "Salina Creek (Sevier River)"), the trail turned southwest following the [Sevier](/wiki/Sevier_River "Sevier River"), [Santa Clara](/wiki/Santa_Clara_River_(Utah) "Santa Clara River (Utah)"), [Virgin Rivers](/wiki/Virgin_River "Virgin River") to the north bank of the Colorado River. There they could follow the Colorado River to Las Vegas Wash, then south through the [Eldorado Valley](/wiki/Eldorado_Valley "Eldorado Valley") and [Piute Valley](/wiki/Piute_Valley "Piute Valley") to join the Mojave Trail, west of the [Mohave](/wiki/Mohave_people "Mohave people") villages (below modern [Laughlin](/wiki/Laughlin,_Nevada "Laughlin, Nevada")) and followed the route between the springs along the Mojave Trail to Soda Lake and the Mojave River. Later caravans could alternatively follow the Armijo Route diverting southwestward from the Colorado at Las Vegas Wash, to Resting Springs and to the Mojave River where it joined the Wolfskill/Yount Route, following that river upward to and over the [San Bernardino Mountains](/wiki/San_Bernardino_Mountains "San Bernardino Mountains") through Cajon Pass, Crowder Canyon and lower Cajon Canyon and across the coastal valleys to Mission San Gabriel and Los Angeles.[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7)[[8]](#cite_note-npsstudy-8)[[11]](#cite_note-Archaeology-11): 11–12

### Northern Branch

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Northern Branch")]

The North Branch of the Old Spanish Trail was established by traders and trappers using Indian and Spanish colonial routes. It ran from Santa Fe north to [Taos](/wiki/Taos,_New_Mexico "Taos, New Mexico") and on north into the [San Luis Valley](/wiki/San_Luis_Valley "San Luis Valley") of Colorado. Caravans then headed west to today's [Saguache](/wiki/Saguache,_Colorado "Saguache, Colorado"), crossing over the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide_of_the_Americas "Continental Divide of the Americas") at [Cochetopa Pass](/wiki/Cochetopa_Pass "Cochetopa Pass"), and then through present day [Gunnison](/wiki/Gunnison,_Colorado "Gunnison, Colorado") and [Montrose](/wiki/Montrose,_Colorado "Montrose, Colorado") to the [Uncompahgre Valley](/wiki/Uncompahgre_Valley "Uncompahgre Valley"). The trail then followed the [Gunnison River](/wiki/Gunnison_River "Gunnison River") to today's [Grand Junction](/wiki/Grand_Junction,_Colorado "Grand Junction, Colorado"), where the Colorado River was forded, and then on west to join the Main Northern Route just east of the Green River. The North Branch later became an interest of explorers seeking viable routes for a transcontinental railroad along the 38th parallel. In 1853 alone, three separate expeditions explored the North Branch over Cochetopa Pass. These groups were led, in order, by [Lieutenant Edward Fitzgerald Beale](/wiki/Edward_Fitzgerald_Beale "Edward Fitzgerald Beale"), [Captain John Williams Gunnison](/wiki/John_Williams_Gunnison "John Williams Gunnison"), and John C. Frémont.[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7)[[8]](#cite_note-npsstudy-8)

### Modifications to the Trail before 1844

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modifications to the Trail before 1844")]

Use of the Old Spanish Trail between 1829 and 1848 resulted in numerous variations as travelers adopted or blazed easier paths. But regardless of the route taken, the Old Spanish Trail crossed several mountain ranges, passed through dry sections with limited grass and sometimes limited water, crossed two deserts, and was often littered with the bones of horses that had died of thirst.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The western portions of the Old Spanish Trail could only be used semi-reliably in winter when rains or snows deposited water in the desert. In summer, there was often no water and the oppressive heat could kill. A single round trip per year was about all that was feasible. After 1848, the western parts of the trail were used for winter access between Utah and California when other trails were closed by snow.[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7)[[8]](#cite_note-npsstudy-8)

#### Lower Narrows Crossing – Cajon Pass Cutoff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lower Narrows Crossing – Cajon Pass Cutoff")]

Sometime before 1844, perhaps as early as 1830, a cut off developed on the Old Spanish Trail that cut the distance traveled along the upper Mojave River, by cutting across what is now [Victor Valley](/wiki/Victor_Valley "Victor Valley"), from the Cajon Pass to a [crossing](/wiki/Lane%27s_Crossing "Lane's Crossing") just below the [Lower Narrows](/wiki/Lower_Narrows_(Mojave_River) "Lower Narrows (Mojave River)") of the river. On April 20, 1844, following the advice of his guide, [John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont") intercepted this route to the river, riding east southeast from [Lake Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_Lake_(Los_Angeles_County,_California) "Elizabeth Lake (Los Angeles County, California)"), north of the [San Gabriel Mountains](/wiki/San_Gabriel_Mountains "San Gabriel Mountains").[[13]](#cite_note-Fremont-13): 258–259

#### Salt Spring – Fork of the Road Cutoff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Salt Spring – Fork of the Road Cutoff")]

Another cutoff to the Armijo route of the Old Spanish Trail had developed before 1844, where the trail forked northeastward from the Mojave River and Mohave Trail, east of what is now [Yermo, California](/wiki/Yermo,_California "Yermo, California"), running up [Spanish Canyon](/wiki/Spanish_Canyon "Spanish Canyon") over [Alvord Mountain](/wiki/Alvord_Mountain "Alvord Mountain"), to [Bitter Spring](/wiki/Bitter_Spring_(San_Bernardino_County) "Bitter Spring (San Bernardino County)"), then through [Red Pass](/wiki/Red_Pass "Red Pass") to join the Armijo route near Salt Spring in the [Silurian Valley](/wiki/Silurian_Valley "Silurian Valley"). Frémont also used this route in 1844.[[13]](#cite_note-Fremont-13): 261–264 The fork of the trails there on the Mojave River, later became known as [Fork of the Road](/wiki/Fork_of_the_Road "Fork of the Road").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Frémont's Cutoff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Frémont's Cutoff")]

One last modification to this route was that followed by John C. Frémont eastward in 1844. His expedition left the Armijo Route at Resting Spring and turned northeastward after crossing the [Nopah Range](/wiki/Nopah_Range "Nopah Range") through Emigrant Pass, through California Valley and across [Pahrump Valley](/wiki/Pahrump_Valley "Pahrump Valley") to Stump Spring and into the mountains to [Mountain Springs](/wiki/Mountain_Springs,_Nevada "Mountain Springs, Nevada"), to [Cottonwood Spring](/wiki/Cottonwood_Spring_(Blue_Diamond,_Nevada) "Cottonwood Spring (Blue Diamond, Nevada)"), to [Las Vegas Springs](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas"). He then crossed the dry 50 miles to the [Muddy River](/wiki/Muddy_River_(Nevada) "Muddy River (Nevada)") before rejoining the Main Route on the Virgin River at [Halfway Wash](/wiki/Halfway_Wash_(Nevada) "Halfway Wash (Nevada)") after crossing what later became known as [Mormon Mesa](/wiki/Mormon_Mesa "Mormon Mesa"). This route saved the large distances caused by the diversion of the Armijo and Main routes to follow the Colorado River, and would later become the route of the [Mormon Road](/wiki/Mormon_Road "Mormon Road"), the wagon road through southern Nevada between Salt Lake City and Los Angeles.[[11]](#cite_note-Archaeology-11): 12

## Historic preservation and commemoration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Historic preservation and commemoration")]

In 1988, a section of the trail in [Arches National Park](/wiki/Arches_National_Park "Arches National Park") was placed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Old_Spanish_Trail_Marker%2C_Las_Vegas%2C_Nevada.jpg/250px-Old_Spanish_Trail_Marker%2C_Las_Vegas%2C_Nevada.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Spanish_Trail_Marker,_Las_Vegas,_Nevada.jpg)

Old Spanish Trail Marker, Las Vegas, Nevada, near Whitney Mesa.

In 2001, the section of the Trail that runs across Nevada from the Arizona border to California was placed on the National Register of Historic Places as the Old Spanish Trail/Mormon Road Historic District.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

[Mojave National Preserve](/wiki/Mojave_National_Preserve "Mojave National Preserve") and [Mojave Trails National Monument](/wiki/Mojave_Trails_National_Monument "Mojave Trails National Monument") preserve sections of the trail traveling through the Mojave Desert in California.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The Old Spanish Trail became the fifteenth [national historic trail](/wiki/National_historic_trail "National historic trail") after Congress adopted Senate Bill 1946 and [President George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") signed the legislation in December 2002.[[7]](#cite_note-warren-7)

Although few traces of the early traders' trail remain, the Trail is now commemorated in many[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] local street and road names, and numerous[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] historical markers in the states that it crossed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Portions of [US 160](/wiki/US_160 "US 160") in Colorado and [US 191](/wiki/US_191 "US 191") in Utah are similarly designated.

[![Modern sign marking the Old Spanish Historical Trail across Pahrump Valley on the California-Nevada border. South Nopah Range in background.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Old_Spanish_Trail_near_Nevada-California_border.jpg/960px-Old_Spanish_Trail_near_Nevada-California_border.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_Spanish_Trail_near_Nevada-California_border.jpg)

Modern sign marking the Old Spanish Historical Trail across Pahrump Valley on the California-Nevada border. South Nopah Range in background.
